cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to tackle this problem step by step. First, let me make sure I understand the problem correctly. We have four students, each given a different number from a set S of four consecutive two-digit integers. The teacher told them that some number in S is divisible by 6, and another different number in S is divisible by 7. Initially, when asked if they could deduce S, all four students said "no." But after hearing that all four said "no," each student was able to figure out the set S. We need to find all possible values of the greatest element in S and sum them up. Alright, so first, let me break down the problem. First, S is four consecutive two-digit integers. Let me denote them as {n, n+1, n+2, n+3} where n is a two-digit number (i.e., 10 ≤ n ≤ 97, since n+3 has to be two-digit, so n ≤ 97). Additionally, in S, there is one number divisible by 6 and a different number divisible by 7. So exactly one number divisible by 6 and exactly one number divisible by 7, and they are different numbers. So they could be overlapping in some way? Wait, but if a number is divisible by both 6 and 7, then it would be divisible by 42. But since numbers are consecutive, it's possible only if such a number is present. But since 42 is two-digit, but the four consecutive numbers can't have two numbers divisible by both 6 and 7. But the problem says "some number... divisible by 6 and a different number... divisible by 7." So there must be at least one divisible by 6, at least one divisible by 7, and they are different numbers. So maybe there could be more than one number divisible by 6 or 7, but at least one of each.
